title: DAY 1 — “The Hanging and the Snow”
text:
Part I · ~20 min spoken
Tonight’s question: How does a homeless mason end up building the most beautiful church in England?

[slow, almost a whisper]
England. The year 1123. A small market town. Cold morning — the kind of cold where your breath shows.
A crowd has gathered around a wooden gallows. They’ve come to watch a hanging. The man on the platform is a stranger — a Frenchman, they say. A jongleur. A wandering minstrel, the kind who travels from village to village with a fiddle and a few songs. Nobody really knows what he did. The official charge is theft. A jewelled chalice, taken from a church.
He’s a tall man. Red hair. Foreign-looking. His hands tied behind his back.
In the crowd, three men are watching him die.
[pause]
The first is a knight. Big, hard man. He looks bored.
The second is a young monk. He won’t quite meet the prisoner’s eyes.
The third is a priest. Black robes. Sharp, hungry face. He watches the hanging the way another man might watch a tradesman finish a job.
And at the back of the crowd — there’s a girl.
[slow]
Young. Hooded. Red hair under the hood. Pregnant. Six, seven months along. Her eyes have not moved from the man on the platform.
[pause]
The hangman kicks the stool out.
The man on the rope kicks once. Twice. Then stills.
[pause]
And the girl — quietly, almost gently — looks at each of the three men in turn. The knight. The monk. The priest. And she says, loud enough that everyone hears:
[whisper, but cold]
“I curse you. I curse you to a slow death from a hidden sickness. I curse you with sorrow that lasts your whole life. I curse the men you become.”
Then she turns, and she walks into the forest.
And nobody — not the knight, not the monk, not the priest, not the crowd — nobody ever sees her again.
[pause, full beat]
That is the first scene of the book. You don’t find out who the dead man was, or who the girl was, or why those three men really put him there, for another eight hundred pages.
But every single thing that happens next — every death, every betrayal, every stone laid in a cathedral that hasn’t even been built yet — flows from that morning.
[pause]

Okay. Twelve years later.
We meet a man. Tom Builder. That’s not his name, it’s his trade — Tom the Builder. He’s a master mason. Big man, kind face, calloused hands. The kind of man who can look at a pile of stones and see a wall, look at a wall and see a building, look at a building and see God.
And he has a dream. One dream. Tom Builder has spent his entire working life dreaming of building a cathedral.
Not a house. Not a chapel. A cathedral. With towers. With arches. With a roof so high a man inside it feels small in a holy way. He’s never done it. Most masons never get to. There aren’t that many cathedrals being built.
Tom is currently building a house. A rich man’s house. His wife Agnes is heavily pregnant, due any week now. His son Alfred — big, slow lad, takes after his father in size if not in heart. His little daughter Martha — small, watchful, kind.
And one morning, the man paying for the house rides up and says: I’ve changed my mind. I don’t want the house anymore.
He pays Tom for the work done. Tom counts the coins. It’s enough for maybe two weeks.
And then it’s winter.
[pause]
You have to understand how dangerous unemployment was in 1135. Masons don’t save — they live job to job. There’s no charity that scales. The roads are dirt tracks between forests. In those forests there are wolves, and outlaws, and the kind of cold that kills.
Tom puts his tools on his back. Agnes carries the smallest child when she can’t walk anymore. They walk from village to village. Tom asks at every monastery, every manor, every parish church — do you need a mason? And every answer is no.
By December, they’re starving.
[slow]
Now I want you to picture this scene. Because this is the scene that breaks Tom Builder, and the whole book turns on it.
It’s snowing. They’re somewhere in the middle of a forest, miles from the nearest village. Agnes goes into labour. Right there. In the snow.
Tom builds her a shelter out of branches. He starts a fire. Alfred fetches water. Martha holds her mother’s hand and cries quietly, because she’s a child and she doesn’t know what else to do.
The baby comes. A boy. Healthy. Crying.
But Agnes — Agnes won’t stop bleeding.
[pause]
Tom watches his wife die in the snow. There’s nothing he can do. He’s a builder. He builds walls. He can’t build a wall against this.
She dies holding the baby.
[pause, full beat]
And here’s where Tom makes the choice that the rest of the book will judge him for. He has no milk. He has no money. He has two children to keep alive. He has miles of forest in every direction. And he has a newborn who needs feeding within hours or he’ll die.
Tom buries Agnes under stones in the snow. He lays the baby on her grave, wrapped in his own cloak. And he walks away. With Alfred. With Martha. He tells himself the baby will die quickly, in the cold, and won’t suffer.
He cannot bring himself to look back.
[whisper]
But somebody is watching.
[pause]
In the trees, twenty paces away, two pairs of eyes have seen this whole scene. A woman — wild-looking, dressed in leather and fur, hair tangled. And a boy beside her, eleven or twelve, with bright red hair.
The woman is called Ellen. The boy is called Jack.
Ellen lives in the forest. Outlawed years ago for reasons no one explains yet. She raises her son alone, hunts her own food, knows every track and every clearing for fifty miles.
She watches Tom walk away. She watches the baby on the grave.
And then, from the other direction down the path — a priest comes walking. With a goat on a lead. A wandering priest. He hears a baby crying. He stops. He picks the baby up. He looks around — sees no one — and walks on, with the baby tucked into his cloak.
Ellen and Jack watch the priest carry Tom’s son away.
Tom never knows. Tom walks on. Tom believes his son is dead.
[pause]
That night, Tom can’t sleep. He turns around. He goes back. He has to know. He has to see the baby’s body. He has to bury him properly.
And when he gets back to Agnes’s grave — the baby is gone.
Tom thinks: wolves. Wolves took him.
And he sinks to his knees in the snow next to his wife’s grave, and finally — finally — Tom Builder weeps.
[pause]
That’s when Ellen steps out of the trees.
[pause, almost a smile in the voice]
Tom doesn’t know what she saw. He doesn’t know about the priest. He doesn’t know his son is alive. What he knows is that a strange wild woman has appeared in the snow, with a red-haired boy behind her, and she’s looking at him not with pity but with — something else. Recognition, almost. Like she’s been waiting for him.
She gives them food. Bread, dried meat — the kind of food the family hasn’t seen in weeks. She lets them sleep by her fire.
In the morning, Tom asks her: Do you know where a builder can find work?
And Ellen says: Walk west. Three days. There’s a town called Kingsbridge. They have a cathedral. The prior is old. The cathedral is falling down. They’ll need a builder soon.
[pause]

Let’s leave Tom on the road with two children, a wild woman, and a red-haired boy he doesn’t yet love. And let’s go meet the other man who matters.
[brisker, shift gears]
Prior Philip.
Philip is Welsh. Small dark man. Thirty-five. Devout. Genuinely, deeply, simply devout — he believes in God the way other men believe in their own hands.
He’s the prior of a tiny monastic cell out in the forest — eight monks, a vegetable garden, a chapel. Quiet life. Philip runs it so well that they’re actually self-sufficient, which most monasteries are not.
One evening his brother arrives. Francis. Francis is a clerk in the household of a powerful earl — close to the court, close to the king. And Francis has heard something he shouldn’t have. He’s heard that one of the great barons of England is plotting to overthrow King Stephen and put another claimant on the throne.
Civil war is coming. The kind that levels villages.
Francis can’t go to the authorities himself — he’d be killed. He tells Philip. He says: Tell someone you trust in the Church. Get word to the king.
Philip thinks. Who does he trust? He thinks of a young, ambitious priest he’s met once — a man named Waleran Bigod. Sharp face. Black robes. Hungry eyes. Philip rides off to find him.
[pause]
Now — hold that name. Waleran Bigod. Just hold it. You’re going to hate him eventually.
[pause]
Philip finds him. Tells him about the plot. Waleran listens carefully. Thanks him. Says he will handle it.
Philip rides home feeling that he has done his duty before God and king.
He has no idea what he’s just set in motion.
[pause]
Some weeks later, Philip is summoned to the cathedral town of Kingsbridge. The old prior has died. The monks need a new prior. They’ve heard about the Welsh monk who runs his little cell so well. They want him.
Philip arrives at Kingsbridge for the first time. He’s elected prior. He’s now in charge of a cathedral.
A cathedral which is, in his honest professional opinion, half-rotten. The roof is sagging. The walls have cracks you could put your fist into. He looks at it and thinks: This building is going to fall down in ten years, and I have no money to fix it.
[pause]
That same night — that very night — Tom Builder, with Agnes dead behind him and a wild woman beside him and two children and a red-haired stepson, walks into Kingsbridge for the first time. Late. Cold. Hungry.
He goes to the priory. He asks the new prior — Philip — for work. Any work.
Philip is sympathetic. He’s seen Tom’s tools. He can tell Tom is a master. But Philip has no money. He apologises. He gives Tom a meal. He says: I’m sorry. I cannot help you.
Tom thanks him. He leads his family back to the cathedral porch, where they curl up under their cloaks. Another night. Another no. Tomorrow they walk on.
Tom sleeps.
[pause]
At some point in the night — Tom wakes up.
The air smells wrong.
[slow]
There’s a glow. An orange, flickering glow. He sits up. He looks at the cathedral above him.
The roof is on fire.
[pause]
By morning, half the cathedral is rubble. Smoking, blackened rubble. Nobody knows how it started. A candle, maybe. A spark. An act of God.
The monks stand in the ruins and weep.
Philip — new prior, three days into the job — stands in the ashes and realises that the building he was supposed to keep standing has just come down around him.
And next to him — quietly, calmly — stands a master mason.
Who has been dreaming of building a cathedral his entire life.
Who happens to be standing in the exact spot where one needs to be built.
Tom Builder looks at the ruined stones. His eyes fill with tears — but they’re not sad tears. They’re something else.
[whisper]
He starts to smile.
[pause, full beat]

[normal voice]
That’s where we stop tonight.
Tomorrow: Tom gets the job he’s been waiting his whole life for. A seventeen-year-old noblewoman called Aliena has everything taken from her in one night, and swears revenge in a way that will redraw the map of England. A villain you will love to hate steps onto the page. And the priest with the hungry eyes — Waleran Bigod — turns out to have used Philip’s secret for purposes Philip cannot yet imagine.
Sleep on that.




The two whispered curse lines are your “magic moments.” Rehearse those out loud at least twice. Everything around them is just transport — those are the destination.
The Agnes death scene — resist the urge to rush. Snowfall, branches, the fire, Martha’s hand. Picture it as you talk; your friend will picture it too.
The Kingsbridge fire ending — the whole 20 minutes is engineered to land on Tom’s smile. Don’t smile yourself when you say “he starts to smile” — say it flat. Let your friend’s imagination do the work.
If you forget a line, don’t recover by speeding up. Slow down. Storytelling forgives a pause; it doesn’t forgive a panicked rush.

title: DAY 3 — “Building”
text:
Part III · ~20 min spoken
Last time: Aliena, twenty years old, having lost everything at seventeen, has clawed her way to wealth as a wool merchant. Her father is dead. Her brother is a knight. And she has just stopped her cart at the top of a valley and looked down at the town of Kingsbridge, where a half-built cathedral is climbing into the sky.
Tonight’s question: What kind of man builds a cathedral, and what kind of man burns one down?

[brisker, opening]
Aliena rides into Kingsbridge.
She goes to the priory. She asks to see the prior. Philip — older now, in his forties, grey starting at his temples — receives her in the chapter house. She tells him: I want to set up my wool business here. I need to rent a stone warehouse. I need to live in the town. And I need the priory’s protection.
Philip is wary at first. A woman, unmarried, wanting to set up a business — this is not what 12th-century English towns are organised around. But Philip has spent twenty years in administration and he can read a balance sheet. He knows what a wool merchant of Aliena’s reputation means for his town. More traffic. More tolls. More tenants. More taxes.
He says yes.
She rents from him. She builds a warehouse. She moves Richard’s small household into a house near hers. And she gets to work.
[pause]
And here’s the thing that nobody quite saw coming. Within a year, the town starts to grow around Aliena. Sellers come to her instead of to the merchants of Winchester. Farmers from a thirty-mile radius find that they can get better prices in Kingsbridge than they can anywhere else. Buyers from Flanders, hearing about her, route their ships and pack-trains through her warehouse. The annual Kingsbridge wool fair, which used to be a sleepy thing, starts to swell.
And as the trade flows in, money flows to the priory. Tolls. Rents. Pilgrim offerings. Suddenly Philip has a budget for the cathedral that he never had before.
The cathedral starts to rise faster.
[pause]

[shift gears, slow]
Now I want to talk about Jack.
Jack is seventeen when Aliena moves to Kingsbridge. He’s been carving stone for three years. He has surpassed every journeyman on the project. He has surpassed his stepfather Tom in fine work — not in design, not in load-bearing engineering, but in carving. He can sculpt the faces of saints into stone in a way that makes them look like they want to speak.
Jack is also strange. He grew up in the forest. He has no friends his own age — he didn’t grow up around any. He reads, which most people his age cannot. He has opinions about God that are mildly heretical. And he is, quite suddenly, in love with Aliena.
[slow]
She doesn’t know.
She walks past the cathedral scaffolding one day, on the way to her warehouse, and Jack — eighteen years old now — stops his chisel mid-stroke and just watches her go by. The other masons see this. They laugh at him. Jack doesn’t care.
He starts carving women’s faces into the capitals of columns. Just for himself. The faces all look like her. Everybody in Kingsbridge can see it. Everybody knows. Aliena is the only person in the town who does not realise that a brilliant young sculptor is carving her face into the walls of a cathedral.
[pause]
She’s twenty-six now. He’s eighteen. To her, he is a boy. A talented boy, yes — she’s not blind, she’s seen his work. But a boy. She is a wealthy widow-in-spirit-if-not-in-law. She isn’t thinking about love. She isn’t thinking about any man, because the only man she has ever known intimately was William Hamleigh in her father’s hall, and the memory of that has built a wall in her that no man can yet reach.
So Jack watches. And carves. And waits.
[pause]

[brisker]
Now — Alfred.
Tom’s actual son. Twenty-seven, twenty-eight by now. Senior journeyman on the project. Heir-apparent to the master mason title when Tom retires. Competent. Hard worker. Big strong hands. Limited imagination.
And Alfred — for years now — has been watching his red-haired stepbrother do work he himself cannot do. Watching Tom praise Jack. Watching the apprentices learn from Jack instead of from him. Watching Jack carve capitals that pilgrims come specifically to see, while Alfred lays plain ashlar walls.
Alfred hates Jack. Not loudly. Not all the time. Just in the way that a steady drip of resentment hates the thing it cannot match.
One afternoon, in the lodge — the workshop where the masons eat their midday meal — Alfred takes a swing at Jack. Just like that. No warning. They go down in the sawdust. Tom has to pull them apart. There’s blood. There’s a broken nose. There are no apologies.
That night, Tom sits Alfred down and tries to talk to him man-to-man. It doesn’t take. Alfred is not a man who can be talked to.
[pause]
I want you to hold all three of these threads. Jack, twenty, in love with Aliena and dreaming of stone. Aliena, twenty-six, wealthy, untouched, hauling fleeces. Alfred, twenty-eight, resentful, dangerous, watching the woman he wants prefer his brother.
Those three are going to crash into each other in a way that will break all of them.
[pause]

[slow, gear shift to William]
Meanwhile — William Hamleigh. Earl of Shiring. About thirty now.
Picture this man. He has had everything his mother promised him. The earldom. The land. The castles. The bannermen. He is — by every external measure — successful. His mother has even arranged a marriage for him.
And he is a wreck.
Two things have gone wrong for William inside himself. The first: he is terrified of hell. Genuinely. He cannot sleep some nights. He confesses constantly. He pays priests to pray for his soul. He believes — correctly — that he is going to burn.
The second: he is impotent with his wife. They have been married four years. There are no children. There will be no children. He does not say it out loud, but he knows it, and his mother knows it, and every servant in the household knows it.
And the rage that those two things produce in William — the rage that he is a damned man with no heir — that rage turns outward. Onto his serfs. Onto the villages he rides through. Onto every woman his men can drag in front of him to prove he is still a man. He is, by Year Six of his earldom, the most feared man in the county, in the way only a small frightened cruelty can be feared.
[pause]
And his hatred for Aliena — who refused him, who survived him, who is now rich in the town next to his — that hatred is the cleanest emotion in his life.
[pause]

So William makes his big move.
[brisker]
He rides to the king. He pays a substantial fee. He requests a royal charter for a wool fair at Shiring. Annual. Held the same week as the wool fair at Kingsbridge.
The king grants it.
This is direct economic warfare. The foreign buyers can only attend one fair. Half of them will go to Shiring. The Kingsbridge fair will be cut in half. The cathedral’s income will collapse.
Philip rides to court to fight it. He gets some concessions — different week, maybe — but the Shiring fair stands.
The Kingsbridge wool fair happens anyway. It’s smaller than last year. But it happens. Stalls go up. Bunting flies. Foreign buyers haggle over fleeces. Aliena is at the centre of it, in her booth, doing the biggest single day of trade of her career.
[pause]
And in the afternoon, William rides into the town with thirty mounted men.
[pause, slow]

I want you to picture this.
A market square. Hundreds of people. Wooden stalls. Bunting. Sheep. Stacks of fleeces stretched out under canvas awnings. Children running between the legs of merchants. The smell of roasting meat from food stalls. The shout of buyers and sellers and the bleating of animals.
And thirty men on horseback, in helmets, with swords drawn, come riding through the gates.
The first thing they do is set fire to the awnings.
[pause]
Canvas burns fast. The fire jumps from stall to stall. The fleeces — fleeces are oily, fleeces are flammable — go up like brushwood. Within minutes there is a wall of fire across the centre of the fair.
Now the crowd panics. Hundreds of people, packed into a small square, with fire to their north and horsemen with swords to their south. They stampede toward the only opening — the cathedral porch, the half-built west front of the cathedral itself.
Children fall and are trampled. Old men trip on stones. A woman is crushed against the priory wall by the press of bodies. William’s men ride through the crowd cutting at anyone who tries to organise.
[pause]
In the middle of this, Tom Builder is standing in the cathedral porch. He’s an old man now — sixty, maybe — but he’s the master mason and the cathedral is his. He’s trying to push people through the porch in an organised way, to keep more children from being trampled, when he sees William’s men starting to set fire to the scaffolding of the cathedral itself.
If the scaffolding burns, the cathedral burns.
[slow]
Tom Builder leaves the porch. He walks out into the burning square. He goes after William.
He gets to William’s horse. He grabs the bridle. He shouts something — we don’t know what, we never know what.
And William — already swinging — brings his sword down on Tom’s head.
[pause, full beat]
Tom Builder. Master mason. Has been dreaming his whole life of building a cathedral. Has been one quarter of the way through building one. Dies in the smoke of a wool fair, in front of the half-finished west front, killed by a small frightened man on a horse.
[pause]

When William rides out, the square is on fire and bodies are everywhere. Philip and Jack and Aliena run out of the smoke trying to find people. The cathedral’s scaffolding has caught in two places — Jack and the apprentices, soaking themselves in water from the priory well, climb up to put it out, while it’s still burning, and they save it.
But Tom is dead.
[pause]
Philip kneels in the ashes and prays. Ellen — Tom’s wife, although they were never married before the Church — kneels next to him and weeps. Martha — Tom’s daughter, eighteen now — kneels next to her. Alfred — Tom’s son — stands frozen.
And Jack — Tom’s stepson, the boy Tom picked up out of the forest and made into a mason — Jack stands next to Tom’s body, and then turns, and looks up at the half-built cathedral, and starts directing the apprentices on what to do next.
The work, in Jack’s hands, does not stop.
[pause]
That same evening, in a room above her warehouse, Aliena sits with her brother Richard. She has been at the fair all day. She has seen things she will not tell anyone about. She looks at Richard and she says:
“I want William Hamleigh dead. By any means. I will pay for it.”
Richard nods.
[pause]

[normal voice]
That’s where we stop tonight.
Tomorrow: Tom is buried. Someone has to be master mason of the cathedral now — and the two candidates are Tom’s son, and Tom’s stepson, and they hate each other. Jack and Aliena finally see each other, and what they see is dangerous. A young woman who has built her whole life around the rescue of her brother makes the worst trade of her life — and a young man who has spent his life waiting for one woman walks out of England with nothing but his tools.
Sleep on that.




The Jack-watching-Aliena-walk-by beat — slow this one. It’s the most romantic moment in the book, and it’s silent. He stops, he watches, she doesn’t see. Don’t oversell it.
The wool fair fire — this is your second great setpiece (after Agnes in the snow). Build it slowly: market square, awnings, fleeces, children. The horsemen arrive almost as an interruption. The fire is fast; the panic is fast; but the setup should breathe.
Tom’s death — flat delivery. Do not editorialise. “He gets to William’s horse. He grabs the bridle. He shouts something — we don’t know what. And William brings his sword down on Tom’s head.” That’s the rhythm. No flourishes.
Closing image — Aliena, that night, telling Richard she wants William dead. Drop volume on her line. She’s been quiet for so long. Now she’s quietly declaring war.

title: DAY 5 — “The Mystery”
text:
Part V · ~20 min spoken
Last time: Aliena married Alfred to buy her brother an army. Alfred could not consummate the marriage and began beating her. She gave birth to Jack’s son in secret. Jack — heartbroken — walked out of England with his tools and a question. Alfred’s stone ceiling collapsed onto the cathedral floor. And Prior Philip, standing in the rubble, said quietly: “We need Jack.”
Tonight’s question: Who really hanged the man in the prologue, and why?

[brisker, opening]
We follow Jack now. Across the Channel in winter. Through Normandy. He walks. He sleeps in barns and monasteries. He earns small coin where he can carving small things at small churches. He moves south, then east. He has heard there is something he needs to see, just outside Paris.
He arrives at the abbey of Saint-Denis in 1147 or thereabouts.
[slow]
Now you need to know what this place is. Saint-Denis is an old royal abbey, a few miles north of Paris. The Kings of France are buried there. The current abbot is a man named Suger — an extraordinary administrator and an even more extraordinary visionary. Suger has just finished rebuilding the choir and the west front of the abbey church.
And it does not look like any building in Europe.
[pause]
Jack walks through the front doors. He looks up. And he sees something he cannot at first understand.
The walls of the choir are not really walls. They are frames. Frames around enormous panels of coloured glass. Stained glass — reds, blues, golds — covering most of the upper part of the church. The light comes through them and falls onto the stone floor in shifting pools of colour.
The arches are not rounded. They are pointed. Where two of them cross, the ceiling is held up by a rib — a stone spine that takes the weight to a single point. Which means the wall underneath doesn’t have to do the work. Which means the wall can be thin — and full of glass.
[pause]
Jack stands in the choir of Saint-Denis and starts to cry.
Because he sees, all at once, that everything he has been building in Kingsbridge is the old way. Romanesque. Thick walls. Tiny windows. Heavy. Dark. The cathedral he has been working on his entire adult life is — by comparison to what is in front of him — a cave.
What is in front of him is light made into a building.
He walks out of the church. He sits on a low wall in the courtyard. He sits there for two hours, not moving. And then he gets up and goes to find the master mason of Saint-Denis and asks if he can stay and work.
[pause]

[brisker]
Jack stays at Saint-Denis for about a year. He works. He learns. Pointed arch — invented to redistribute load. Rib vault — for the same reason, and to free the walls. Flying buttress — the external scaffolding of stone that catches the outward thrust of high walls, so the walls themselves can be made of glass.
These three innovations, taken together, are the start of what we now call Gothic architecture. Almost nobody in England has seen them yet. Jack is one of the first English masons to learn how they actually work.
He copies plans into a small leather-bound book. He sketches details. He talks to other masons who have worked at Chartres, at Sens, at Noyon. He has — without knowing it — apprenticed himself to the future.
[pause]
But he has not forgotten the other question. The question that drove him out of England in the first place.
Who was my father?
He asks. Carefully. A man named Jack. A jongleur. Came to England with a delegation in 1123. Hanged. Did anyone here know him?
For months, nothing. Then one night, in a tavern in Paris, an old man — really old, eighty maybe, blind in one eye — hears Jack ask the question and says: I knew him.
[pause, slow]
The old man’s name is not important. What matters is what he tells Jack.
He says: Your father’s name was Jacques de Cherbourg. He was a jongleur. A minstrel. A storyteller. He went to England in the household of an embassy from the King of France. There was an incident at the court of the English king. A jewelled chalice — a gift from the French king to the English king — went missing the night the embassy arrived. The English wanted someone to blame. The French wanted to leave with their honour intact. Three men of the English court — a knight, a young monk, a priest — produced the chalice and produced Jacques and said he had stolen it. He was hanged the next morning in a small market town on the way back to the coast. The embassy was let go. Everyone said the matter was settled.
The old man looks at Jack across the tavern table and says:
[whisper]
Your father did not steal that chalice. The three men who hanged him stole it.
[pause]

[slow]
Jack rides south. He goes to Spain. Specifically to Toledo — at this point, one of the most extraordinary cities in Europe. Christian, Muslim, and Jewish scholars working together on translation. Arab mathematics flowing into Latin. Greek philosophy, preserved by Arab scholars during the Dark Ages, being translated into the languages Western Europe can read.
Jack is at Toledo for two years. He learns geometry that almost no English mason knows. He learns to use the astrolabe. He learns proportional drawing. He becomes, slowly, the most technically educated master mason in England — although nobody in England knows it yet, because he isn’t in England.
And he keeps asking about the chalice. About the embassy of 1123.
A Spanish bishop he is working for — building a small church — connects him to a French clerk who connects him to an old Norman knight who, twenty-five years later, remembers the affair.
The knight — drunk, in a tavern, at last — gives him the three names.
[pause]
The knight who produced the chalice was Percy Hamleigh.
The young monk who corroborated was a man named James — who became, in time, Prior James of Kingsbridge. The old prior whom Philip succeeded.
The priest who actually planned it, who took the chalice in the first place, was Waleran Bigod.
[pause]
[slow]
Now Jack sits with this. Slowly.
Percy Hamleigh. William’s father. Long dead now. Untouchable.
Prior James of Kingsbridge. Dead before Philip arrived. Untouchable.
But Waleran Bigod is alive. Waleran Bigod is the Bishop of Kingsbridge. Waleran Bigod has been at war with Philip for twenty years. Waleran Bigod is the priest Aliena’s son might one day kneel before to receive confirmation.
And Waleran Bigod was one of the three men whom — twenty-five years ago — a sixteen-year-old pregnant girl on the edge of a market crowd cursed in a voice loud enough for the whole town to hear.
[pause, full beat]

[brisker]
Jack starts walking back to England. It is, by now, 1150.
He has been gone four years.
He arrives in Kingsbridge on an afternoon in early summer.
[slow]
The town has changed. It is bigger. The cathedral is — strangely — almost the same as he left it. Alfred’s choir collapse stalled the work badly. After that disaster the priory ran out of money and the project has been crawling. The roof of the choir is timber, hurriedly thrown up to keep the rain off. The nave is unfinished. The west front is half-built.
Jack walks through the town. Nobody recognises him at first — he is bearded, thinner, sun-darkened from Spain. He goes to his mother Ellen’s cottage on the edge of the forest. He knocks. She opens the door.
She does not speak. She holds him for a long time. Then she says: Aliena left Alfred two years ago. She lives in the priory guest house. She has been waiting for you.
[pause]
He doesn’t go to her that day. He goes to Philip first.
[pause]

[brisker]
He tells Philip the whole story. The chalice. The three men. Waleran.
Philip — who has been at war with Waleran for two decades and never quite known why Waleran was so willing to lie, so willing to cheat, so willing to burn villages to win — Philip listens with his hands flat on his desk.
When Jack finishes, Philip says: I believe you. But the law does not work on hearsay from a tavern in Toledo. We need proof. The chalice itself, ideally. Or witnesses who can testify in a Church court.
Jack says: I will find them.
Philip says: Yes. Take your time. He’s not going anywhere.
Then Philip says: Now. Tell me what you learned in France.
[pause]
And Jack — who has not yet been to see Aliena, who has not yet met his son, who has been back in Kingsbridge for less than a day — Jack sits with Philip across that desk and starts drawing pointed arches on parchment.
Philip looks at the drawings. Looks at Jack. And Philip — the slowest, most cautious, most administrative man in the building — Philip says:
[whisper]
We will build the most beautiful cathedral in England.
[pause]

[slow]
Now — Jack and Aliena.
It is the next morning. Aliena is in the priory guest house. She has known for an hour, by gossip running through the town, that Jack is back. She is sitting on her bed, in her best dress, with her hands shaking.
He knocks. She opens. He stands in the doorway.
Behind her, in the room, a five-year-old red-haired boy looks up.
Jack looks at him. He looks at Aliena. He says, quietly:
Tommy?
Aliena nods.
[pause]
Jack does not hug her. Not yet. He goes to the boy first. He kneels. He says: My name is Jack. I have been away a long time. I would very much like to be your friend.
Tommy looks at his mother, who is now silently weeping. Tommy looks back at Jack. Tommy holds out a small wooden carving — a horse, that Jack had carved for him before he left, when Tommy was a baby and Aliena had hidden it from Alfred.
[pause]
That’s the moment. Not the kiss. Not the embrace. The boy holding out the carving.
[pause]

[brisker, picking up]
The annulment is not difficult. Aliena testifies that her marriage to Alfred was never consummated. Two physicians examine her and confirm — by tests we will not go into — that she has had a child but has not had a husband, biologically speaking. Alfred himself, when called to testify, admits it, because he is by now broken and ashamed and would rather be free of her than fight for her.
Waleran tries to block the annulment on procedural grounds. He fails. Even bishops cannot block annulments forever when the evidence is this clear.
Aliena and Jack are married — quietly, by Philip, in the small priory chapel, on a Tuesday morning in the autumn of 1150.
Tommy is at the front. Ellen is at the front. Martha — Tom’s daughter, still unmarried, devoted to Jack since they were children — is at the front.
And then they get back to work.
[pause]

[slow, building]
Jack becomes master mason. He redesigns the cathedral.
The nave will have pointed arches. The triforium will be glazed. There will be flying buttresses outside, taking the weight of the high vaults. The clerestory will be enormous — long bands of stained glass, twenty feet tall.
He starts by demolishing Alfred’s choir entirely. The temporary timber roof comes off. The walls — built for Romanesque weight — are reinforced. New piers are dropped to support the rib vaults Jack is planning. Stone starts to come from the quarry again, in volume.
And — slowly, slowly — over the next several years — a different building begins to rise out of the foundations Tom Builder dug.
A Gothic building.
The first of its kind in England.
The light through the new clerestory, when the first windows are glazed, falls onto the floor of the nave in pools of red and blue and gold. The townspeople of Kingsbridge stand at the back of the church on the first Sunday it is opened to them and cannot quite work out what they are seeing.
Jack stands at the back of the church too, with Aliena’s hand in his, and Tommy in front of them, and what he is thinking is —
[whisper]
Two of the three are dead. One is left.
[pause, full beat]

[normal voice]
That’s where we stop tonight.
Tomorrow: Henry, the son Empress Matilda swore her barons to support, finally takes the throne as King Henry II. The civil war ends. The king picks his best friend Thomas Becket to be Archbishop of Canterbury and lives to regret it. William Hamleigh, getting old, finally answers for the wool fair and for forty years of cruelty. And the priest with the hungry eyes from the very first scene of the book — twenty-eight years after a sixteen-year-old girl cursed him in a market square — finds out what a curse, properly aged, can do to a man.
Sleep on that.




Saint-Denis arrival — the moment when Jack walks in and sees Gothic for the first time is one of the great moments in the book. Slow this all the way down. He doesn’t speak. He just looks. Then he sits on the wall. Then he cries.
The old man in the tavern — drop volume to a near whisper for “Your father did not steal that chalice.” This is the central revelation of the book and it’s delivered by an unknown old man over wine. Honor that quietness.
The three names — pause between each. Percy. James. Waleran. Each one a beat.
Tommy with the wooden horse — don’t oversell it. Children meeting their fathers are one of those scenes that destroys itself if you tilt sentimental. Flat delivery, let the image do the work.
The closing whisper — “Two of the three are dead. One is left.” This is your hand-off to Day 6. Like a fuse being lit.
